title: The AI Tool Landscape
goal: Pick the right AI tool for each job and know how to drive it.

blocks.html:
AI tools come in a few flavors. Knowing which does what saves you hours of forcing the wrong one.

  • Chat tools (ChatGPT, Claude) — you type, they answer. Best for writing, summarizing, and quick help.
  • Assistants — AI built into an app you already use (email, docs), helping with that app's work.
  • Agents — you give a goal and they take several steps on their own. Powerful, but they need watching.
  • Image and video tools — for making visuals, not text.

blocks.question: You want to turn one blog post into five short social posts. Which tool fits best?

options.explain: Exactly right. Reshaping text you already have into new formats is what chat tools do best.
Those make pictures, not written posts.
Overkill, and not built for marketing copy.
Wrong medium — you want text, not video.

blocks.html: The same task can go to a simple chat tool or a do-it-all agent. They are not equally safe. Read both ends, then reveal the rule of thumb.

left.label: A chat tool
left.text: You type, it answers, one turn at a time. You drive every step and read every result.

right.label: An AI agent
right.text: You give a goal and it takes several steps on its own — but it can wander off and needs checking.

why: A chat tool is the simplest and safest — great for drafts, answers, and rewrites.
An 'assistant' sits in the middle: built into an app (email, docs) to help with that app's tasks.
An agent tries to do a whole job end to end — powerful, but it's the least reliable and needs a human watching.
Rule of thumb: use the simplest tool that does the job. Reach for an agent only when a task is truly multi-step, and still check its work.

lessons.title: Match a Real Task to a Tool

blocks.html: Your turn. Pick what you'd reach for and see how it plays out. There's no penalty — try a few paths.

start.text: A customer left a long, angry review. You want a calm, professional reply. What do you reach for?

choices.label: Paste it into a chat tool and ask for a calm draft reply
Send it to an autonomous agent to handle the whole complaint

chat.text: You paste the review into a chat tool and ask for a calm, professional reply.
chat.outcome: Good call. It drafts a measured reply in seconds — and you read and tweak it before it ever goes out. Simplest tool, right job.

agent.text: You hand the whole complaint to an autonomous agent.
agent.outcome: Risky. An agent might reply, offer a refund, or escalate on its own — with an angry customer, one wrong autonomous step makes it worse. Keep a human in the loop for anything customer-facing.

image.outcome: Wrong medium — that makes pictures, not replies. Back to a chat tool for this one.

blocks.html: Most owners need just a few tools, not fifty. Tick the ones you'll actually set up — that's your starter kit.

items.text: One general chat tool (ChatGPT or Claude) for writing, summarizing, and answers.
One assistant where you already work (your email or docs).
One image tool for quick graphics — only if you need visuals.
A saved list of your best prompts (you're already building one here).
